ach_question: What is the best-supported cause of the uncontained engine failure and fire?

hypotheses.label: Internal manufacturing anomaly caused disk fatigue
hypotheses.claim: The engine failure was caused by low-cycle fatigue cracks initiated from an internal subsurface manufacturing anomaly in the HPT stage 2 disk.

hypotheses.label: Airline maintenance missed a required defect
hypotheses.claim: The incident primarily resulted from American Airlines or maintenance personnel missing a defect that required inspection should have found.

hypotheses.label: Flight crew action caused the loss
hypotheses.claim: The rejected takeoff or evacuation decision was the primary cause of the incident severity.

hypotheses.label: Independent airframe or fuel-system defect caused the fire
hypotheses.claim: A separate wing, fuel-line, or airframe failure independent of the disk rupture was the best-supported cause of the fire.

evidence_rows.label: Probable cause names internal anomaly
evidence_rows.text_anchor: The HPT stage 2 disk failed because of low-cycle fatigue cracks that initiated from an internal subsurface manufacturing anomaly
evidence_rows.expected_relevance: Direct, central support for h1 and the intended pivotal sensitivity row.

evidence_rows.label: Metallurgy found discrete dirty white spot
evidence_rows.text_anchor: multiple low-cycle fatigue cracks that initiated from an internal material anomaly, known as a discrete dirty white spot
evidence_rows.expected_relevance: Direct support for h1; corroborates the mechanism independently of the probable-cause sentence.

evidence_rows.label: Fragment caused fuel-fed fire
evidence_rows.text_anchor: severing the main engine fuel feed line and breaching the right-wing fuel tank, which led to a fuel-fed fire
evidence_rows.expected_relevance: Directly links disk rupture to the fire, weakening h4 as an independent-cause explanation.

evidence_rows.label: No prior structural or system failure
evidence_rows.text_anchor: No evidence indicated any structural, engine, or system failures before the uncontained engine failure occurred
evidence_rows.expected_relevance: Counters h4 and limits support for a preexisting non-disk failure theory.

evidence_rows.label: Required surface inspection could not detect cracks
evidence_rows.text_anchor: not detectable at the time of the HPT stage 2 disk's last inspection using the surface-based inspection techniques
evidence_rows.expected_relevance: Weakens h2 by explaining why required inspection did not reveal the cracks.

evidence_rows.label: Captain rejected takeoff properly
evidence_rows.text_anchor: the captain made a timely decision to reject the takeoff and performed the maneuver in accordance with company training and procedures
evidence_rows.expected_relevance: Counters h3 and keeps crew action off the causal center.

evidence_rows.label: Ultrasonic inspection would have helped
evidence_rows.text_anchor: If a subsurface inspection technique, such as an ultrasonic inspection, had been required
evidence_rows.expected_relevance: Double-edged: it supports a prevention avenue while not proving airline maintenance fault under then-required procedures.

evidence_rows.label: Maintenance background
evidence_rows.text_anchor: the engine had accumulated 19,139 hours and 3,057 cycles
evidence_rows.expected_relevance: Background scope row; relevant to maintenance context but not sufficient to make h2 best.

expected_read.rationale: The report explicitly identifies an internal subsurface manufacturing anomaly and low-cycle fatigue as the cause, while maintenance and crew alternatives are countered or only prevention-related.
expected_read.flip_note: Removing e1 should not make h2 or h3 win outright, but it removes the clearest official cause statement and should sharply weaken the expected winner; e2 then becomes the next strongest support.
